---
title: "Bias and small-signal amplifiers"
description: "Establish a transistor operating point, derive its local incremental behavior, and design bounded voltage-gain, follower, and bias-current stages."
chapter-id: A03
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter develops a complete instructional path from DC bias to
single-transistor gain, loading, headroom, frequency limits, and a guarded
design decision. Calculations, illustrative parameter sweeps, and executable
arithmetic are identified as such; they are not measurements. Physical
amplifier evidence belongs in aligned Lab **L07**. The chapter remains subject
to technical and editorial revision. See the
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) for the meaning of status levels.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for amplifier work"}
Practical work in this chapter is limited to **current-limited,
extra-low-voltage** sources, approved components, local supervision, and an
approved procedure. Set the source current limit before connecting the circuit,
de-energize before changing wiring, and check electrolytic-capacitor polarity
and voltage rating before energizing. Stop for smoke, odor, discoloration,
unexpected rapid temperature rise, or loss of current limiting. This chapter
does not authorize mains-connected amplifiers, high-energy batteries, RF power
amplifiers, loudspeaker power stages, medical interfaces, or connections to
unbounded external signals [@iec61010].
:::

## Central question

> How can a nonlinear transistor amplify a small changing signal predictably?

A transistor's collector or drain current is a curved function of its terminal
voltages. Yet a correctly biased stage can reproduce a small input waveform at
larger voltage or current, while a stage made from the same transistor can clip,
drift, or stop amplifying after a modest change in temperature, load, or device.
The apparent contradiction disappears when the DC operating point and the
signal excursion are treated separately [@sedra2020microelectronic].

Before calculating, consider a common-emitter stage connected to a 12 V supply.
Predict what happens if:

- the transistor is replaced by another of the same part number but with twice
  the DC current gain;
- an emitter resistor is added while the base-bias voltage is retained;
- a capacitor bypasses that emitter resistor only at signal frequencies;
- the load resistance is reduced from open circuit to 10 k$\Omega$; and
- the input amplitude is increased until the collector waveform flattens.

A complete answer must make the device region, DC load line, local tangent,
schematic, source and load, frequency-dependent paths, measured test points, and
acceptance requirement agree. Their disagreement is useful evidence: it can
identify a wrong operating region, bias sensitivity, loading, an omitted
capacitance, measurement intrusion, or operation outside the local linear range.

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes [A02](a02-transistors.qmd): BJT and MOSFET terminal
references, large-signal constitutive relations, operating regions, load lines,
ratings, and thermal limits. It also assumes
[F08](../01-foundations/f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd): RMS phasors,
impedance, transfer functions, loading, and frequency response. After completing
the chapter, you should be able to:

- establish and verify a BJT or MOSFET quiescent point using the source network,
  device relation, and DC load line;
- compare fixed, divider, emitter/source-resistor, collector-feedback, and
  current-source bias by their sensitivity, headroom, and loading;
- derive $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_o$ as local derivatives, state their units and
  validity, and distinguish DC $\beta$ from incremental current gain;
- derive loaded gain, input resistance, and output resistance for
  common-emitter, common-source, common-base, common-gate, emitter-follower,
  and source-follower stages;
- explain the useful impedance transformation and the headroom, leakage, and
  capacitance costs of a Darlington connection;
- analyze simple discrete current mirrors as conditional bias elements rather
  than ideal current sources, and explain what a Wilson mirror improves and
  what it costs;
- choose coupling and bypass capacitors, estimate low- and high-frequency
  limits, and identify loading, distortion, and noise mechanisms; and
- turn amplifier requirements into a calculation, an executable cross-check,
  a measurement plan, and an uncertainty-guarded decision.

The only direct prerequisites are A02 and F08. Use
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd) just in time for
Taylor expansion and partial derivatives,
[F05](../01-foundations/f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) for dissipation, and
[F06](../01-foundations/f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) for the guarded
decision.

::: {.callout-note title="Scope boundary — one-transistor gain here, controlled gain next"}
[A02](a02-transistors.qmd) owns transistor physics, switching regions, drive,
safe operating area, switching loss, and inductive protection. This chapter uses
those results to develop DC polarization and local amplification. It introduces
single-stage distortion, bandwidth, and noise only far enough to bound a
transistor stage; [A06](a06-noise-nonlinearity-stability.qmd) owns chain-level
budgets. [A04](a04-op-amps-feedback.qmd) develops general negative-feedback and
operational-amplifier design. [A07](a07-integrated-circuits.qmd) owns precision
mirrors, active loads, differential pairs, process variation, matching, and
layout.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Polarity duals and supply conventions"}
The drawn NPN and nMOS devices conduct conventional current from the positive
supply toward the reference node. The NPN and nMOS mirror circuits shown later
are specifically low-side current sinks. PNP and pMOS stages are polarity duals
that can source current from the positive rail. A split supply can place an
emitter or source near zero while retaining bias headroom. These transformations
do not justify changing signs from memory: redraw the current arrows, declare
every terminal voltage, recheck electrolytic polarity, and solve the DC region
again
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].
:::

## The quiescent point places the device inside a gain region

### Total quantities, DC values, and increments

Retain the terminal references declared in A02. For an NPN BJT,
$V_{BE}=V_B-V_E$ and $V_{CE}=V_C-V_E$, with $I_B$ and $I_C$ entering the base
and collector and $I_E$ leaving the emitter. For an n-channel MOSFET,
$V_{GS}=V_G-V_S$ and $V_{DS}=V_D-V_S$, with $I_G$ and $I_D$ entering the gate
and drain and $I_S$ leaving the source.

The **quiescent point**, or **Q point**, is the DC solution with the intended
signal set to zero. To prevent a lowercase symbol from meaning both a complete
waveform and a perturbation, write a total node voltage as

$$
v_X^{\mathrm{tot}}(t)=V_{XQ}+\widetilde v_X(t),
\qquad
i_X^{\mathrm{tot}}(t)=I_{XQ}+\widetilde i_X(t).
$$ {#eq-a03-decomposition}

Uppercase $V_{XQ}$ and $I_{XQ}$ are constant no-signal DC values. A tilde
denotes an instantaneous departure from that Q point. A first-order response to
a zero-mean sinusoid has zero mean; a nonlinear response need not, because
rectification can shift the average away from the no-signal Q point. For
sinusoidal steady state, this chapter retains F08's $e^{j\omega t}$ convention
and uses underlined RMS phasors, for example $\underline V_o$. A peak amplitude
is written $\widehat V_o$. These are different quantities.

The decomposition in @eq-a03-decomposition is a definition, not an
approximation. The
approximation enters only when a nonlinear device relation is replaced by its
local tangent.

### Divider bias and the DC load line

The common-emitter circuit in @fig-a03-common-emitter is the chapter's canonical
stage. $C_{\mathrm{IN}}$ and $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$ isolate the source and load DC
conditions. $R_{E1}$ remains in the signal path, while $C_E$ can bypass
$R_{E2}$ over a chosen frequency range. At DC steady state, ideal capacitors are
open circuits, so $R_L$, $R_{\mathrm{sig}}$, and all three capacitors are absent
from the bias calculation. The total DC emitter resistance is
$R_E=R_{E1}+R_{E2}$.

![A divider-biased common-emitter amplifier with split emitter degeneration.](../../../build/circuits/a03-common-emitter-amplifier.png){#fig-a03-common-emitter fig-alt="A signal source v sig at TPIN with source resistance R sig drives the base of NPN transistor Q1 through input coupling capacitor C IN. Divider resistors R1 and R2 bias base test point TPB from V CC. Collector resistor R C connects V CC to collector test point TPC. Emitter test point TPE passes through unbypassed R E1 and bypassed R E2 to TP0; capacitor C E is in parallel with R E2. Output coupling capacitor C OUT connects TPC to output test point TPO and load R L." width="96%"}

For every signed stage result, define the node voltage positive at the named
test point relative to TP0:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\mathrm{sig}}&\equiv V(\mathrm{TPIN})-V(\mathrm{TP0}),&
v_b&\equiv V(\mathrm{TPB})-V(\mathrm{TP0}),\\
v_c&\equiv V(\mathrm{TPC})-V(\mathrm{TP0}),&
v_e&\equiv V(\mathrm{TPE})-V(\mathrm{TP0}),\\
v_o&\equiv V(\mathrm{TPO})-V(\mathrm{TP0}).
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding tilde quantities are increments with the same polarities.
All common-emitter voltage gains below are output numerator divided by input
denominator using these references.

Replace the divider by its Thévenin equivalent as seen from TPB:

$$
V_{\mathrm{TH}}=V_{CC}\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2},
\qquad
R_{\mathrm{TH}}=R_1\parallel R_2.
$$ {#eq-a03-bias-thevenin}

The voltage expression is the unloaded divider value. Base current loads the
divider through $R_{\mathrm{TH}}$; calling $V_{\mathrm{TH}}$ the actual base
voltage would silently discard that loading.

For a first calculation, assume forward-active operation, a constant
$V_{BEQ}$, and a constant DC current gain
$\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}=I_{CQ}/I_{BQ}$. KVL from the Thévenin source through the
base-emitter path and emitter resistor gives

$$
V_{\mathrm{TH}}
=I_{BQ}R_{\mathrm{TH}}+V_{BEQ}
+(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)I_{BQ}R_E.
$$

Therefore

$$
I_{BQ}\approx
\frac{V_{\mathrm{TH}}-V_{BEQ}}
{R_{\mathrm{TH}}+(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)R_E},
\quad
I_{CQ}\approx\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}I_{BQ},
\quad
I_{EQ}=(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)I_{BQ}.
$$ {#eq-a03-divider-bias}

The first two equalities are constitutive approximations; the last follows from
the exact terminal-current balance. They are usable only if the resulting
voltages verify forward-active operation. The node voltages are

$$
V_{EQ}=I_{EQ}R_E,\qquad
V_{CQ}=V_{CC}-I_{CQ}R_C,\qquad
V_{CEQ}=V_{CQ}-V_{EQ}.
$$ {#eq-a03-dc-node-voltages}

The DC collector-emitter loop also gives the source/load-line relation

$$
V_{CE}=V_{CC}-I_C R_C-I_E R_E.
$$ {#eq-a03-dc-load-line}

This is KVL and is exact for the indicated lumped DC network. Replacing
$I_E$ by $I_C$ gives the useful large-$\beta$ approximation
$V_{CE}\approx V_{CC}-I_C(R_C+R_E)$. The Q point is where this network relation
intersects the transistor's actual output characteristic. If the calculation
in @eq-a03-divider-bias predicts a point in saturation or cutoff, the assumption
used to obtain it has failed; the network must be solved in the correct region
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

### Bias strategies and sensitivity

Biasing is not merely choosing a nominal current. It is choosing how strongly
that current moves when device parameters, supply, resistance, temperature, or
load change. Common discrete strategies are compared in @tbl-a03-bias-methods
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

| Bias strategy | Stabilizing mechanism | Principal limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed base resistor | Few parts; base current is set approximately by a source and resistor | $I_C$ depends strongly on $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ and $V_{BE}$ |
| Stiff voltage divider | Base voltage is less affected by base current when divider current is large | Divider consumes current; no emitter feedback if $R_E=0$ |
| Divider plus emitter resistor | A current increase raises $V_E$, reducing $V_{BE}$ for a nearly fixed $V_B$ | Uses voltage headroom and reduces gain unless partly bypassed |
| Collector-to-base feedback | A current increase lowers $V_C$, which reduces base drive | Input and output are coupled; available gain and headroom change |
| Source resistor in a MOSFET | A current increase raises $V_S$ and reduces $V_{GS}$ | Uses headroom; result still depends on threshold and transfer curve |
| Current source or mirror | Bias current can be less supply-dependent and can present high incremental resistance | Compliance, matching, temperature, and finite output resistance remain |

: Common discrete bias strategies. “Stabilizing” means reduced sensitivity over
a stated range, not independence from device or environment. {#tbl-a03-bias-methods}

The first three BJT strategies are shown in @fig-a03-bias-strategies. Assume a
constant $V_{BEQ}$, forward-active operation, and constant
$\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ only long enough to compare their sensitivity.

![Fixed, collector-feedback, and divider-plus-emitter BJT bias networks.](../../../build/circuits/a03-bias-strategies.png){#fig-a03-bias-strategies fig-alt="Panel a shows fixed bias: R B connects V CC to the base of an NPN transistor, R C connects V CC to its collector, and the emitter is grounded. Panel b moves R B from V CC to the collector so a collector-voltage decrease opposes base current. Panel c uses divider R1 and R2 at the base and emitter resistor R E so a current increase raises emitter voltage and reduces V BE." width="100%"}

For fixed base bias, the base loop gives

$$
I_{BQ}\approx\frac{V_{CC}-V_{BEQ}}{R_B},
\qquad
I_{CQ}\approx
\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}\frac{V_{CC}-V_{BEQ}}{R_B}.
$$ {#eq-a03-fixed-bias}

The collector current is directly proportional to the assumed $\beta$. The
collector network still limits feasible current; this equation must not
override its load line.

For collector-to-base feedback, current through $R_C$ supplies both collector
and base currents. The collector-node balance and base loop give

$$
\frac{V_{CC}-V_C}{R_C}=I_C+I_B
=(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)I_B,
\qquad
I_B=\frac{V_C-V_{BEQ}}{R_B}.
$$

Eliminating $V_C$ gives

$$
I_{CQ}\approx
\frac{\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}(V_{CC}-V_{BEQ})}
{R_B+(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)R_C}.
$$ {#eq-a03-collector-feedback-bias}

If current rises, the drop across $R_C$ rises, collector voltage falls, and the
base current through $R_B$ falls. That is the feedback path. It also couples
the signal output node to the input and uses collector-voltage headroom. The
divider-plus-emitter circuit adds feedback through $R_E$ while allowing the
collector to serve as a less directly loaded output
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

The feedback in an emitter resistor is visible in @eq-a03-divider-bias;
multiplying by $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ gives

$$
I_{CQ}\approx
\frac{\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}(V_{\mathrm{TH}}-V_{BEQ})}
{R_{\mathrm{TH}}+(\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}+1)R_E}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bias-sensitivity}

As $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}\rightarrow\infty$,

$$
I_{CQ}\longrightarrow
\frac{V_{\mathrm{TH}}-V_{BEQ}}{R_E}.
$$ {#eq-a03-beta-limit}

The finite limit is revealing: emitter feedback can make the current much less
sensitive to $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$. It does not remove sensitivity to
$V_{\mathrm{TH}}$, $V_{BEQ}$, $R_E$, temperature, or resistor tolerance. If
$R_E\rightarrow0$, the stabilizing term disappears and
$I_C\approx\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}(V_{\mathrm{TH}}-V_{BE})/R_{\mathrm{TH}}$ until a
region boundary invalidates that expression.

Temperature changes both junction injection and current gain. Over a limited
operating range, a silicon BJT at roughly constant collector current often has
a negative base-emitter temperature coefficient near a few millivolts per
kelvin, but that coefficient is operating-point dependent rather than a
universal constant. A falling $V_{BE}$ at fixed base voltage tends to raise
current; $R_E$ makes the resulting rise in emitter voltage oppose it
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic]. Thermal stability still requires
checking device dissipation and the thermal path from F05.

### Worked example: divider-bias sensitivity

Use the circuit of @fig-a03-common-emitter with these **illustrative nominal**
values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CC}&=12.0~\mathrm V, &
R_1&=82.0~\mathrm{k}\Omega, &
R_2&=15.0~\mathrm{k}\Omega,\\
R_C&=4.70~\mathrm{k}\Omega, &
R_{E1}&=270~\Omega, &
R_{E2}&=680~\Omega.
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume $V_{BEQ}=0.700$ V and $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}=100$ only for the nominal
screen. These are assumptions, not guaranteed 2N3904 values. Using
the Thévenin result in @eq-a03-bias-thevenin,

$$
V_{\mathrm{TH}}
=12.0~\mathrm V\frac{15.0}{82.0+15.0}
=1.856~\mathrm V,
$$

and

$$
R_{\mathrm{TH}}
=82.0~\mathrm{k}\Omega\parallel15.0~\mathrm{k}\Omega
=12.68~\mathrm{k}\Omega.
$$

With $R_E=950~\Omega$,

$$
I_{BQ}\approx
\frac{1.856-0.700}
{12.68~\mathrm{k}\Omega+101(950~\Omega)}
=10.64~\mu\mathrm A.
$$

The relations @eq-a03-divider-bias and @eq-a03-dc-node-voltages then give

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{CQ}&\approx1.064~\mathrm{mA},&
V_{EQ}&\approx1.021~\mathrm V,\\
V_{CQ}&\approx7.000~\mathrm V,&
V_{CEQ}&\approx5.979~\mathrm V.
\end{aligned}
$$

The current scale is about 1 mA, the collector is near the middle of a 12 V
supply, and $V_{CEQ}$ is far from the low-voltage switching state. Those are
useful sanity checks, not proof for all devices and temperatures.

Holding the illustrative $V_{BEQ}$ fixed while changing
$\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ from 70 to 200 moves $I_{CQ}$ only from 1.010 mA to
1.135 mA. With $R_E=0$ the same algebra would predict 6.38–18.2 mA, and the
12 V, 4.70 k$\Omega$ collector network has an ideal-short current intercept of
only $12~\mathrm V/4.70~\mathrm{k}\Omega=2.55$ mA. Every value in the predicted
range is therefore impossible in forward-active operation. The load line has
rejected the assumption.

### MOSFET polarization with source feedback

The common-source stage in @fig-a03-common-source uses the same strategy with
different device physics. The gate divider establishes an approximate DC
voltage

$$
V_{GQ}\approx V_{DD}\frac{R_{G2}}{R_{G1}+R_{G2}}
$$

only when gate leakage and contamination currents are negligible relative to
divider current. At DC,

$$
V_{GSQ}=V_{GQ}-I_{DQ}R_S,\qquad
V_{DSQ}=V_{DD}-I_{DQ}(R_D+R_S).
$$ {#eq-a03-mos-bias}

![A divider-biased common-source amplifier with source degeneration.](../../../build/circuits/a03-common-source-amplifier.png){#fig-a03-common-source fig-alt="A signal source v sig at TPIN and resistance R sig drive the gate of n-channel MOSFET M1 through C IN. R G1 and R G2 bias gate test point TPG from V DD. Drain resistor R D connects V DD to drain test point TPD. Source test point TPS passes through R S to TP0. C OUT couples TPD to output TPO and load R L." width="94%"}

For the common-source stage, define $v_g$, $v_d$, $v_s$, and $v_o$ positive
at TPG, TPD, TPS, and TPO relative to TP0. Define $v_{\mathrm{sig}}$ at TPIN
relative to TP0. The tilde increments retain those polarities.

For the long-channel square-law approximation introduced in A02,

$$
I_D\approx\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS}-V_{\mathrm{th}})^2
$$

in MOSFET saturation. Substituting @eq-a03-mos-bias produces an equation in
$I_{DQ}$ that must be solved and then checked against
$V_{DSQ}\ge V_{GSQ}-V_{\mathrm{th}}$. Here *MOSFET saturation* is the
controlled-current region used for common-source gain; it is not the
low-voltage BJT saturation state. Real discrete MOSFET threshold, transfer
curve, body effect, and channel-length modulation can make the square-law
solution inaccurate. A source resistor reduces, but does not eliminate, that
dependence [@sze2006physics; @sedra2020microelectronic].

### Worked example: square-law bias and region verification

Consider an **illustrative long-channel calculation**, not a claim about a
named transistor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DD}&=10.0~\mathrm V,&
R_{G1}&=R_{G2}=1.00~\mathrm{M}\Omega,\\
R_D&=3.30~\mathrm{k}\Omega,&
R_S&=1.00~\mathrm{k}\Omega,\\
k_n&=2.00~\mathrm{mA/V^2},&
V_{\mathrm{th}}&=2.00~\mathrm V.
\end{aligned}
$$

Negligible DC gate current gives $V_{GQ}=5.00$ V. Define the dimensionless
number $x\equiv I_{DQ}/(1~\mathrm{mA})$. Because 1 mA through 1 k$\Omega$
produces 1 V, substitution into the square law gives

$$
x=(3.00-x)^2.
$$

The algebraic roots are $x=1.697$ and $x=5.303$, corresponding to currents
1.697 mA and 5.303 mA. The larger root would make
$V_{GSQ}-V_{\mathrm{th}}<0$ and is extraneous to the assumed conduction branch.
For the physical candidate,

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{SQ}&=1.697~\mathrm V,&
V_{GSQ}&=3.303~\mathrm V,\\
V_{\mathrm{OV}}&=1.303~\mathrm V,&
V_{DQ}&=4.400~\mathrm V,\\
V_{DSQ}&=2.703~\mathrm V.
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $V_{DSQ}=2.703$ V exceeds $V_{\mathrm{OV}}=1.303$ V, the nominal
candidate verifies the assumed MOSFET saturation region. The corresponding
square-law transconductance is

$$
g_m=k_nV_{\mathrm{OV}}
=(2.00~\mathrm{mA/V^2})(1.303~\mathrm V)
=2.61~\mathrm{mS}.
$$

With $R_L=10.0$ k$\Omega$, $R_{\mathrm{sig}}=10.0$ k$\Omega$, and
$r_o\rightarrow\infty$, the common-source result derived later gives
$A_{v,g}\approx-1.80$. The gate divider presents 500 k$\Omega$, so source
attenuation is $500/(500+10)=0.980$ and
$A_{v,\mathrm{sig}}\approx-1.76$.

The sensitivity screen is more important than the nominal answer. If the same
square-law calculation uses $V_{\mathrm{th}}=1.50$ V, it proposes
$I_D=2.06$ mA, but then $V_{DS}=1.13$ V is **less** than
$V_{\mathrm{OV}}=1.44$ V. The assumed saturation relation fails and the circuit
must be solved in the ohmic region. A fixed gate voltage plus source feedback
is therefore not a guarantee against threshold spread; the actual
condition-matched device relation still decides the Q point.

## Local linearization turns curvature into incremental parameters

### The tangent at the Q point

Let a memoryless terminal relation near the Q point be $I=F(V)$. With
$V=V_Q+\widetilde v$, Taylor expansion gives

$$
I_Q+\widetilde i
=F(V_Q)
+\left.\frac{\mathrm dF}{\mathrm dV}\right|_Q\widetilde v
+\frac{1}{2}
\left.\frac{\mathrm d^2F}{\mathrm dV^2}\right|_Q\widetilde v^2+\cdots.
$$ {#eq-a03-taylor}

Because $I_Q=F(V_Q)$, the first-order approximation is

$$
\widetilde i\approx g_m\widetilde v,
\qquad
g_m\equiv
\left.\frac{\partial I_{\mathrm{out}}}
{\partial V_{\mathrm{control}}}\right|_Q.
$$ {#eq-a03-gm-definition}

$g_m$ is **transconductance**, measured in ampere per volt, or siemens. It is a
local slope, not a stage voltage gain. The approximation is valid only when the
quadratic and higher terms are acceptably small over the actual signal
excursion, while the device remains in the same operating region. A “small”
signal is therefore small relative to the local curvature and headroom, not
merely small in everyday units [@sedra2020microelectronic].

For a multivariable output relation $I=F(V_1,V_2)$, the first-order increment is

$$
\widetilde i\approx
\left.\frac{\partial F}{\partial V_1}\right|_Q\widetilde v_1+
\left.\frac{\partial F}{\partial V_2}\right|_Q\widetilde v_2.
$$ {#eq-a03-total-increment}

This is why both a controlled current and a finite output resistance appear in
the incremental equivalents of @fig-a03-incremental.

![Low-frequency BJT and MOSFET incremental equivalents about a Q point.](../../../build/circuits/a03-incremental-equivalents.png){#fig-a03-incremental fig-alt="Panel a shows a BJT hybrid-pi equivalent with r pi from base to emitter, a downward controlled current g m v pi from collector to emitter, and output resistance r o in parallel. Panel b shows a MOSFET equivalent with an open low-frequency gate control port, voltage v gs from gate to source, a downward controlled current g m v gs from drain to source, and output resistance r o in parallel." width="96%"}

The dependent-source directions in @fig-a03-incremental match the A02
convention: positive $\widetilde v_\pi=\widetilde v_b-\widetilde v_e$ increases
conventional collector current from collector to emitter, and positive
$\widetilde v_{gs}=\widetilde v_g-\widetilde v_s$ increases conventional drain
current from drain to source.

### BJT incremental parameters

Using A02's forward-active exponential approximation

$$
I_C\approx I_S\exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{nV_T}\right),
\qquad V_T=\frac{kT}{q_e},
$$

differentiation at the Q point gives

$$
g_m\equiv
\left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\right|_Q
=\frac{I_{CQ}}{nV_T}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bjt-gm}

At $T=300$ K, $V_T\approx25.85$ mV. For the common ideality approximation
$n=1$ and $I_{CQ}=1.064$ mA,

$$
g_m\approx
\frac{1.064~\mathrm{mA}}{25.85~\mathrm{mV}}
=41.2~\mathrm{mS}.
$$

The dimensional check is
$\mathrm A/\mathrm V=\mathrm S$. Doubling $I_{CQ}$ doubles $g_m$ in this
approximation, but also changes power, headroom, capacitance, and usually
current gain.

Define the incremental common-emitter current gain at the same Q point and
frequency as

$$
\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}\equiv
\frac{\widetilde i_c}{\widetilde i_b}.
$$

Then the hybrid-$\pi$ input resistance is

$$
r_\pi\equiv
\frac{\widetilde v_\pi}{\widetilde i_b}
=\frac{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}}{g_m}.
$$ {#eq-a03-rpi}

The DC datasheet quantity $h_{FE}=I_C/I_B$ is not automatically
$\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$. They are ratios defined by different perturbations and
test conditions. If $\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=100$ is used illustratively with the
nominal Q point, $r_\pi\approx2.43$ k$\Omega$.

::: {.callout-note title="Reading legacy BJT small-signal notation"}
Older texts, instruments, and datasheets often use hybrid parameters.
Uppercase $h_{FE}$ commonly denotes a static ratio $I_C/I_B$, while lowercase
$h_{fe}$ commonly denotes an incremental forward current ratio at a stated
Q point and frequency. Under the unilateral common-emitter approximation,
$h_{ie}\approx r_\pi$, $h_{fe}\approx\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$, and
$h_{oe}\approx1/r_o$; reverse parameter $h_{re}$ is then neglected. These are
conditional mappings, not identities across test circuits or frequency.
Always use the manufacturer's exact definition, conditions, and letter case
[@malvino2021electronic; @onsemi2n3904; @sedra2020microelectronic].
:::

The emitter-side T-equivalent resistance

$$
r_e\equiv\frac{r_\pi}{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1}
\approx\frac{1}{g_m}
$$ {#eq-a03-re}

is a local equivalent quantity, not the physical resistor $R_E$. Here
$1/g_m\approx24.3~\Omega$.

### MOSFET incremental parameters

For a MOSFET in its gain region, definitions are more durable than a particular
square-law formula:

$$
g_m\equiv
\left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right|_Q,
\qquad
g_o\equiv
\left.\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}}\right|_Q,
\qquad
r_o\equiv\frac{1}{g_o}.
$$ {#eq-a03-mos-incremental}

Under the long-channel square-law approximation,

$$
g_m=k_nV_{\mathrm{OV}}
=\frac{2I_{DQ}}{V_{\mathrm{OV}}},
\qquad
V_{\mathrm{OV}}\equiv V_{GSQ}-V_{\mathrm{th}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-mos-gm}

This special case shows a useful trend: more drain current or less overdrive
raises transconductance. It is not a universal modern-MOSFET law. Mobility
reduction, short-channel effects, body effect, temperature, and the actual
transfer curve can dominate; for a real design, obtain $g_m$ from a
condition-matched curve, simulation description, or local measurement
[@sze2006physics; @sedra2020microelectronic].

At low frequency, an intact insulated gate has very high incremental input
resistance. That does not mean infinite stage input impedance: the gate-bias
network remains connected, leakage exists, and gate-source and gate-drain charge
produce finite frequency-dependent current.

### Finite output resistance

In forward-active BJT operation, base-width modulation makes collector current
increase somewhat with $V_{CE}$ at fixed $V_{BE}$. In MOSFET saturation,
channel-length modulation produces the analogous dependence on $V_{DS}$
[@sze2006physics]. A local empirical description is

$$
\widetilde i_o
=g_m\widetilde v_{\mathrm{control}}
+g_o\widetilde v_{\mathrm{output}},
\qquad r_o=\frac{1}{g_o}.
$$ {#eq-a03-output-conductance}

For the common BJT Early-voltage fit,

$$
r_o\approx\frac{V_A+V_{CEQ}}{I_{CQ}}
\approx\frac{V_A}{I_{CQ}}
\quad\text{when }V_A\gg V_{CEQ}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bjt-ro}

$V_A$ is a fitted parameter for the stated device, bias, and temperature, not a
universal constant. The limiting case $r_o\rightarrow\infty$ recovers an ideal
controlled current source. A finite $r_o$ lowers gain and changes output
resistance.

## Voltage-gain stages trade polarity, loading, and feedback

### Common-emitter gain with degeneration

At a frequency where $C_{\mathrm{IN}}$ and $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$ have negligible
series impedance and $C_E$ effectively bypasses $R_{E2}$, the ideal supply has
zero incremental voltage and is therefore AC reference. The unbypassed
$R_{E1}$ remains. The full transformation from physical circuit to incremental
network is shown in @fig-a03-ce-midband. It retains $r_o$ so the learner can see
where the first-order approximation later omits it.

![Complete midband incremental network for the canonical common-emitter stage.](../../../build/circuits/a03-common-emitter-midband.png){#fig-a03-ce-midband fig-alt="An incremental source v sig and R sig drive base node b. R B equal to R1 parallel R2 connects b to AC reference. Hybrid-pi resistance r pi connects b to emitter node e, which returns through unbypassed R E1. Collector node c returns through R C and load R L to AC reference. Output resistance r o and a downward controlled current g m v pi connect c to e. Nodes b, c, and e correspond to TPB, TPC, and TPE." width="98%"}

For the following closed-form derivation, take $r_o\rightarrow\infty$ and
neglect reverse transfer. Define the loaded collector resistance

$$
R_C'\equiv R_C\parallel R_L.
$$ {#eq-a03-loaded-collector}

With $\widetilde v_\pi=\widetilde v_b-\widetilde v_e$, the dependent source
gives $\widetilde i_c=g_m\widetilde v_\pi$. Since
$\widetilde i_b=\widetilde v_\pi/r_\pi$,

$$
\widetilde i_e
=\widetilde i_c+\widetilde i_b
=\left(g_m+\frac{1}{r_\pi}\right)\widetilde v_\pi.
$$

The base increment is therefore

$$
\widetilde v_b
=\widetilde v_\pi+\widetilde i_eR_{E1}
=\widetilde v_\pi
\left[1+\left(g_m+\frac{1}{r_\pi}\right)R_{E1}\right].
$$

The collector increment is
$\widetilde v_c=-g_m\widetilde v_\pi R_C'$. Thus the midband
base-to-collector voltage gain is

$$
A_{v,b}\equiv\frac{\widetilde v_c}{\widetilde v_b}
\approx
\frac{-g_mR_C'}
{1+\left(g_m+1/r_\pi\right)R_{E1}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-ce-gain}

The negative sign follows the declared polarities: an increase in base voltage
raises collector current and increases the drop across $R_C$, so collector
voltage falls. In sinusoidal steady state, the negative midband gain corresponds
to approximately $180^\circ$ phase inversion; it is not by itself a propagation
delay.

For large $\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$,

$$
A_{v,b}\approx-\frac{R_C'}{r_e+R_{E1}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-ce-gain-re}

Two limiting cases teach the design trade:

- if $R_{E1}=0$, $A_{v,b}\approx-g_mR_C'$ and device $g_m$ strongly controls
  gain;
- if $R_{E1}\gg r_e$, $A_{v,b}\approx-R_C'/R_{E1}$, so resistor ratio controls
  gain more strongly, at the cost of smaller magnitude and emitter headroom.

This local emitter feedback is called **degeneration**. It improves gain
predictability and linearity, but it is not the full loop-gain treatment
developed in A04 [@sedra2020microelectronic].

### Input and output resistance

Looking into the transistor base while the emitter sees $R_{E1}$,

$$
R_{\mathrm{in,base}}
=\frac{\widetilde v_b}{\widetilde i_b}
\approx r_\pi+(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)R_{E1}.
$$ {#eq-a03-base-input-resistance}

The stage input at TPB includes the bias divider:

$$
R_{\mathrm{in}}
\approx R_1\parallel R_2\parallel R_{\mathrm{in,base}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-stage-input-resistance}

If a Thévenin source $\widetilde v_{\mathrm{sig}}$ with resistance
$R_{\mathrm{sig}}$ drives TPB through an effectively short
$C_{\mathrm{IN}}$, source loading gives

$$
\frac{\widetilde v_b}{\widetilde v_{\mathrm{sig}}}
=\frac{R_{\mathrm{in}}}
{R_{\mathrm{sig}}+R_{\mathrm{in}}},
$$

and the source-to-output gain is

$$
A_{v,\mathrm{sig}}
\approx A_{v,b}
\frac{R_{\mathrm{in}}}
{R_{\mathrm{sig}}+R_{\mathrm{in}}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-source-gain}

The output resistance is a port definition: set the independent input source to
zero, retain dependent sources, apply a test voltage at the output, and take
$R_{\mathrm{out}}=\widetilde v_t/\widetilde i_t$. With the emitter effectively
fixed and reverse transfer neglected,

$$
R_{\mathrm{out}}\approx R_C\parallel r_o.
$$ {#eq-a03-ce-output-resistance}

Emitter degeneration and transistor reverse effects can change this result.
With finite $r_o$, it connects collector to the moving emitter rather than
collector to AC reference, so it cannot simply be folded into $R_C'$. Use the
complete network in @fig-a03-ce-midband and nodal analysis. The test-source
definition remains valid when a shortcut is not.

### Nominal midband result

Continue the illustrative stage with $R_L=10.0$ k$\Omega$,
$R_{\mathrm{sig}}=600~\Omega$, $\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=100$, and initially neglect
$r_o$. Then

$$
R_C'=4.70~\mathrm{k}\Omega\parallel10.0~\mathrm{k}\Omega
=3.197~\mathrm{k}\Omega.
$$

Using $g_m=41.2$ mS and $r_\pi=2.43$ k$\Omega$ in @eq-a03-ce-gain gives

$$
A_{v,b}\approx-10.77.
$$

The results in @eq-a03-base-input-resistance and the following stage-input
relation, @eq-a03-stage-input-resistance, give

$$
R_{\mathrm{in,base}}\approx29.7~\mathrm{k}\Omega,
\qquad
R_{\mathrm{in}}\approx8.89~\mathrm{k}\Omega.
$$

The source attenuation is $8.89/(8.89+0.600)=0.937$, so

$$
A_{v,\mathrm{sig}}\approx-10.08.
$$

This result is a nominal local prediction. It is not a guarantee of gain
accuracy, distortion, or bandwidth for a physical 2N3904.

### Common-source gain

For @fig-a03-common-source, define

$$
R_D'\equiv R_D\parallel R_L.
$$

At midband, with unbypassed $R_S$, $r_o\rightarrow\infty$, and body effect
neglected,
$\widetilde i_d=g_m\widetilde v_{gs}$,
$\widetilde v_s\approx\widetilde i_dR_S$, and
$\widetilde v_g=\widetilde v_{gs}+\widetilde v_s$. Therefore

$$
A_{v,g}\equiv\frac{\widetilde v_d}{\widetilde v_g}
\approx\frac{-g_mR_D'}{1+g_mR_S}.
$$ {#eq-a03-cs-gain}

Retaining finite $r_o$ in the same low-frequency equivalent gives, by drain and
source nodal analysis,

$$
A_{v,g}
\approx
\frac{-g_mR_D'}
{1+g_mR_S+(R_D'+R_S)/r_o}.
$$ {#eq-a03-cs-gain-ro}

If $g_mR_S\gg1$, the gain approaches $-R_D'/R_S$. If $R_S=0$, it approaches
$-g_mR_D'$. The midband stage input is approximately
$R_{G1}\parallel R_{G2}$, not infinity, and high-frequency input current also
flows through device capacitances. Body effect increases the incremental
source-to-channel control and lowers source-follower or degenerated-stage gain;
finite $r_o$ also lowers gain [@sedra2020microelectronic].

With the source increment fixed at AC reference, a first-order common-source
output resistance is $R_D\parallel r_o$. Source degeneration changes the
transistor-side resistance, so the general result again comes from nulling the
independent input, applying an output test source, and retaining the dependent
source.

### Common-base and common-gate stages accept current at a low-resistance port

Common-emitter and common-source are not the only voltage-gain arrangements.
In @fig-a03-common-base-gate, a named base or gate bias network and an
idealized current sink provide the two DC paths needed to establish the
operating point. The current sink is assumed to have high incremental output
resistance; a physical resistor or mirror would add finite loading and
compliance limits. Meanwhile, $C_B$ or $C_G$ makes the control terminal an AC
reference only over the frequency range where its impedance is sufficiently
small. The signal enters the emitter or source, and the output remains at the
collector or drain.

![Common-base and common-gate stages with separately declared DC bias and AC-reference paths.](../../../build/circuits/a03-common-base-gate.png){#fig-a03-common-base-gate fig-alt="Panel a shows an NPN common-base stage. A source and finite source resistance drive the emitter through an input capacitor, while a downward ideal bias-current sink supplies a DC emitter path. The collector connects through R C to V CC and supplies output voltage. The base connects to a DC bias port and through C B to ground, making it an AC reference at midband. Panel b shows the analogous nMOS common-gate stage with a source input, downward source-bias current sink, drain resistor R D, gate bias port, and gate bypass capacitor C G." width="100%"}

For the BJT, take the base as AC reference, neglect $r_o$, and define
$R_C'=R_C\parallel R_L$. Because
$\widetilde v_\pi=-\widetilde v_e$, a positive emitter-voltage increment
reduces collector current. The collector resistor converts that current
decrease into a positive collector-voltage increment:

$$
A_{v,e}\equiv\frac{\widetilde v_c}{\widetilde v_e}
\approx +g_mR_C'.
$$ {#eq-a03-common-base-gain}

The emitter input resistance and transistor current gain are approximately

$$
R_{\mathrm{in,e}}
\approx\frac{r_\pi}{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1}
\approx\frac{1}{g_m},
\qquad
\alpha_{\mathrm{ac}}
\equiv\frac{\widetilde i_c}{\widetilde i_e}
\approx\frac{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}}{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1}.
$$ {#eq-a03-common-base-input}

Thus the common-base stage is non-inverting and accepts signal current from a
low-resistance source; its transistor current gain is slightly below unity,
even though its voltage gain can exceed one. $R_{\mathrm{in,e}}$ is the
device-port resistance. The source sees it in parallel with the bias source's
finite incremental resistance and any other emitter paths. Consequently, the
available source-to-output gain also includes
$R_{\mathrm{in,total}}/(R_{\mathrm{sig}}+R_{\mathrm{in,total}})$; a large
$g_mR_C'$ does not overcome severe source attenuation.

For the common-gate stage, neglect body effect and $r_o$ first, and define
$R_D'=R_D\parallel R_L$. With the gate at AC reference,
$\widetilde v_{gs}=-\widetilde v_s$, so

$$
A_{v,s}\equiv\frac{\widetilde v_d}{\widetilde v_s}
\approx +g_mR_D',
\qquad
R_{\mathrm{in,s}}\approx\frac{1}{g_m}.
$$ {#eq-a03-common-gate}

Here $R_{\mathrm{in,s}}$ is again the device-port resistance. If the body is
tied to the source, $\widetilde v_{bs}=0$ and there is no body-effect increment.
If the body is instead fixed at AC reference, the corresponding approximations
become $R_{\mathrm{in,s}}\approx1/(g_m+g_{mb})$ and
$A_{v,s}\approx +(g_m+g_{mb})R_D'$. Finite $r_o$, source resistance,
bias-source impedance, and loading require the complete incremental network.

Output resistance also depends on the nulled input termination. Let $R_X$ be
the incremental resistance from the emitter or source node to AC reference,
including the nulled signal source and bias path. Under the same unilateral
low-frequency assumptions,

$$
r_{\mathrm{out,tr,BJT}}
\approx r_o+(1+g_mr_o)(R_X\parallel r_\pi),
$$

and, with the MOS body at AC reference,

$$
r_{\mathrm{out,tr,MOS}}
\approx r_o+\left[1+(g_m+g_{mb})r_o\right]R_X.
$$

The stage output resistances are then screened as
$R_C\parallel r_{\mathrm{out,tr,BJT}}$ and
$R_D\parallel r_{\mathrm{out,tr,MOS}}$. An ideal zero-resistance nulled input
makes the transistor-side first screen $r_o$; a finite termination can raise
it through local feedback. The test-source method remains the authoritative
procedure.

These low-input-resistance stages are specialized when used alone, but they are
important for low-impedance sources and current transfer
[@malvino2021electronic; @sedra2020microelectronic].

#### A cascode is not merely a cascade

A **cascade** connects the output port of one ordinary stage to the input port
of another and must include the interstage loading derived later. A
**cascode** stacks a common-emitter with a common-base device, or a
common-source with a common-gate device, so essentially the same signal current
passes through both, as the BJT example in @fig-a03-bjt-cascode makes explicit.

![A BJT cascode separates transconductance and isolation roles along one signal-current path.](../../../build/circuits/a03-bjt-cascode.png){#fig-a03-bjt-cascode fig-alt="Two NPN transistors are stacked vertically. Lower Q1 receives a combined DC bias and incremental input at its base and returns through emitter resistor R E. Its collector connects directly upward to Q2 emitter at intermediate test point v x, marked as having small signal movement. Q2 base connects to bias V B2 and bypass capacitor C B to AC reference. Q2 collector connects through R C to V CC and horizontally to output v o. Labels identify Q1 as the common-emitter transconductance device and Q2 as the common-base isolation device." width="64%"}

The upper device holds the lower collector or drain
increment comparatively small. That reduces reverse voltage feedback and
Miller multiplication, improves input-output isolation, and can raise output
resistance. The price is another bias voltage, another device, and less
available voltage headroom. A07 develops the complete small-signal resistance,
active-load, matching, and integrated-layout consequences
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

### Voltage gain does not create energy

The signal controlling the transistor does not supply the amplified load
energy. The DC supply does. Over a steady periodic interval, the average stored
energy in ideal capacitors returns to its initial value, so the supply input
power is divided among delivered load power, resistor and device dissipation,
and any other declared output paths. During startup, the capacitors can
accumulate energy, so that whole-cycle steady-state simplification does not yet
apply.

A voltage-gain magnitude above one does not establish power gain unless input
and output impedances and amplitude conventions are included. With RMS
sinusoids and resistive ports,

$$
G_P\equiv\frac{P_L}{P_{\mathrm{in}}}
=\frac{V_{L,\mathrm{RMS}}^2/R_L}
{V_{\mathrm{in,RMS}}^2/R_{\mathrm{in}}}.
$$

This ratio describes the chosen signal ports; it is not an efficiency. An
efficiency denominator must include every energy input over the interval,
including the DC supply and any net release of stored energy
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

All canonical stages in this chapter use **class-A bias**: the device is
intended to remain in its gain region and conduct throughout the complete
signal cycle. It therefore dissipates DC power even with no input signal, and
cutoff or saturation/ohmic-region approach bounds the usable excursion. Class
B, AB, and C operation, complementary output stages, efficiency optimization,
and power-amplifier thermal design require a separate large-signal treatment
[@malvino2021electronic; @sedra2020microelectronic].

## Followers and compound stages transform impedance

As shown in @fig-a03-followers, three common non-inverting stages are useful.
The emitter follower is also called **common collector**, and the source
follower is also called **common drain**, because the named supply-side
terminal is common to input and output in the incremental circuit. In each,
the output is taken from an emitter or source, so increasing the input raises
rather than lowers the output [@malvino2021electronic;
@sedra2020microelectronic].

![Emitter, source, and Darlington followers.](../../../build/circuits/a03-followers-and-darlington.png){#fig-a03-followers fig-alt="Panel a shows an NPN emitter follower with collector at V CC, input at the base, output at the emitter, and emitter resistor to ground. Panel b shows an n-channel MOSFET source follower with drain at V DD, input at the gate, output at the source, and source resistor to ground. Panel c shows two NPN transistors in a Darlington connection, with Q1 emitter driving Q2 base, joined collectors at V CC, output at Q2 emitter, and an emitter resistor to ground." width="100%"}

### Emitter follower

Let $R_E$ be the physical bias resistor shown in @fig-a03-followers, and define
the total loaded incremental resistance
$R_E'\equiv R_E\parallel R_L$. From
$\widetilde v_e=(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)\widetilde i_bR_E'$ and
$\widetilde v_b=\widetilde i_br_\pi+\widetilde v_e$,

$$
A_{v,\mathrm{EF}}\equiv
\frac{\widetilde v_e}{\widetilde v_b}
=\frac{(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)R_E'}
{r_\pi+(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)R_E'}.
$$ {#eq-a03-emitter-follower-gain}

For $(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)R_E'\gg r_\pi$, the gain approaches one from below.
The input resistance at the base is
$r_\pi+(\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1)R_E'$, so a load is reflected upward by roughly
$\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1$. With the load removed and an input Thévenin resistance
$R_B'$ connected to the base, a first-order output-resistance screen is

$$
R_{\mathrm{out,EF}}
\approx
\left[
\frac{r_\pi+R_B'}{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1}
\right]\parallel R_E.
$$ {#eq-a03-emitter-follower-rout}

The follower can therefore accept a signal from a relatively high-resistance
source and drive a lower-resistance load without large voltage gain. Its
available current and output swing remain bounded by bias current, cutoff,
saturation, dissipation, and the source network.

### Source follower

Let $R_S$ be the physical source-bias resistor and
$R_S'\equiv R_S\parallel R_L$. Ignoring body effect and $r_o$ first, the source
follower gives

$$
A_{v,\mathrm{SF}}
\approx\frac{g_mR_S'}{1+g_mR_S'},
$$ {#eq-a03-source-follower-gain}

The gate draws little low-frequency conduction current, but the gate-bias
network and capacitance still define input loading. Body transconductance,
finite $r_o$, source-resistor loading, and limited gate-to-source headroom keep
the gain below the ideal limit [@sedra2020microelectronic].

With the independent gate source nulled, body effect and $r_o$ neglected, a
first-order source-follower output resistance is

$$
R_{\mathrm{out,SF}}\approx\frac{1}{g_m}\parallel R_S.
$$ {#eq-a03-source-follower-rout}

Finite gate-source impedance, body transconductance, and $r_o$ require the
test-source method for a more complete result.

### Darlington connection

In the Darlington stage, $Q_1$ emitter current supplies $Q_2$ base current.
Under the crude constant-gain approximation,

$$
\beta_{\mathrm{D}}
=\frac{I_{C1}+I_{C2}}{I_{B1}}
\approx\beta_1\beta_2+\beta_1+\beta_2.
$$ {#eq-a03-darlington-beta}

The product term can give very high input resistance and current gain. The costs
are approximately two forward base-emitter drops from input to output, more
capacitance and stored charge, leakage multiplication, reduced low-supply
headroom, and parameter dependence. The pair is not one ideal transistor, and
the $\beta_1\beta_2$ rule alone does not establish output current, linearity, or
safe dissipation [@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

## Current mirrors provide conditional bias currents

An amplifier often needs a bias current that changes less with node voltage than
a resistor-fed current. As shown in @fig-a03-current-mirrors, a simple
two-device mirror establishes a shared control voltage in a reference
device and applies it to an output device. The shown NPN and nMOS arrangements
are low-side current **sinks**. PNP and pMOS polarity duals can form high-side
current sources when the load must return toward the lower rail
[@analogdevices2021currentmirrors; @horowitz2015art].

![Rectangular low-side BJT and MOSFET current mirrors used as bias elements.](../../../build/circuits/a03-simple-current-mirrors.png){#fig-a03-current-mirrors fig-alt="Panel a shows inward-facing matched NPN transistors Q1 and Q2 with separate grounded emitters and a short horizontal common-base connection. An orthogonal strap above Q1 ties its collector to its base without crossing either transistor; R REF feeds that reference node from V CC. Q2 collector rises vertically to output TPO. Panel b uses the same rectangular arrangement for matched nMOS devices with common gates and grounded sources." width="96%"}

For the NPN mirror, define $I_{\mathrm{REF}}$ as conventional current downward
through $R_{\mathrm{REF}}$, and $I_O$ as conventional current entering $Q_2$'s
collector. Assume matched devices at the same temperature, equal collector
currents at equal $V_{BE}$, forward-active operation, and equal finite
$\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$. The reference-node KCL is

$$
I_{\mathrm{REF}}
=I_{C1}+I_{B1}+I_{B2}
=I_O\left(1+\frac{2}{\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}}\right).
$$

Thus

$$
I_O\approx
\frac{I_{\mathrm{REF}}}{1+2/\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}},
\qquad
I_{\mathrm{REF}}\approx
\frac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}}{R_{\mathrm{REF}}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bjt-mirror}

As $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}\rightarrow\infty$, $I_O\rightarrow I_{\mathrm{REF}}$.
At $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}=100$, base currents alone make the idealized ratio
$I_O/I_{\mathrm{REF}}\approx0.980$. Mismatch, unequal temperature, unequal
$V_{CE}$, resistor tolerance, and Early effect add error. If output voltage
falls too low, $Q_2$ leaves forward-active operation and the mirror loses
compliance.

For the nMOS mirror, negligible DC gate current removes the base-current error
in the first approximation. Equal $V_{GS}$ gives equal currents only for matched
devices at equal temperature and adequate $V_{DS}$. Threshold mismatch,
channel-length modulation, body connection, and output compliance remain.

### Four quantities keep mirror claims separate

A statement such as “the mirror supplies 1 mA” is incomplete. At least four
quantities must be distinguished:

1. **Current-transfer accuracy** compares $I_O$ with $I_{\mathrm{REF}}$ under
   declared device, temperature, and output-voltage conditions.
2. **Incremental output resistance** holds the reference current fixed and
   measures the local output-port slope,

   $$
   r_{\mathrm{out,mir}}
   \equiv
   \left.\frac{\partial v_O}{\partial i_O}\right|_{I_{\mathrm{REF}},Q},
   \qquad
   \Delta i_O\approx\frac{\Delta v_O}{r_{\mathrm{out,mir}}}.
   $$ {#eq-a03-mirror-output-resistance}

3. **Compliance** is the output-voltage interval over which the device remains
   in the assumed region **and** a stated current-error limit is met.
4. **Matching, thermal, and dynamic limits** describe device spread, unequal
   temperature, capacitance, topology-dependent startup, and frequency
   dependence.

In the simplest first-order model,
$r_{\mathrm{out,mir}}\approx r_o$ for the BJT output device and
$r_{\mathrm{out,mir}}\approx1/(\lambda I_{DQ})$ for the square-law MOSFET with
channel-length modulation. These are local slopes, not load resistors or
guaranteed constant-current ranges. A convenient exercise condition such as
$V_{CE}>0.30$ V is not a universal compliance specification; a defensible
limit must be tied to region evidence and permitted current error
[@analogdevices2021currentmirrors; @sedra2020microelectronic].

### A Wilson mirror demonstrates improvement by feedback

The classic three-NPN Wilson sink in @fig-a03-wilson-mirror is relevant here
because it directly answers two simple-mirror limitations. The lower devices
$Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are matched, $Q_2$ is diode connected, and output device $Q_3$
feeds the shared control node through its emitter. A change at the output is
fed back through the lower pair instead of acting only on one output
transistor.

![Classic three-NPN Wilson current sink with separate reference, control, and output nodes.](../../../build/circuits/a03-wilson-current-mirror.png){#fig-a03-wilson-mirror fig-alt="Three NPN transistors form a Wilson current sink. Matched lower devices Q1 and Q2 have grounded emitters. Their bases share a control node with diode-connected Q2 collector and output-transistor Q3 emitter. Q1 collector joins Q3 base at a separate reference node fed through R REF from V CC. Q3 collector alone rises to output test point TPO, where output current enters and output voltage is positive relative to ground." width="76%"}

Assume equal $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}=\beta$, matched $Q_1$ and $Q_2$,
forward-active operation, and neglect Early effect for the current-transfer
calculation. KCL then gives

$$
\frac{I_O}{I_{\mathrm{REF}}}
=
\frac{1}{1+2/[\beta(\beta+2)]}
\approx 1-\frac{2}{\beta^2}
\quad(\beta\gg1).
$$ {#eq-a03-wilson-ratio}

At $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}=100$, this idealized ratio is about 0.99980,
compared with 0.98039 for the simple two-NPN mirror. Independently, hold the
reference current increment fixed and assume matched small-signal parameters,
$\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}\gg1$, and the usual condition
$g_mr_o\gg\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$. A useful order estimate is

$$
r_{\mathrm{out,Wilson}}\approx\frac{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}r_o}{2}.
$$ {#eq-a03-wilson-output-resistance}

Neither result is a discrete-circuit guarantee. The classic Wilson topology
uses another device and internal node. Its approximate minimum output voltage
is
$V_{O,\min}\approx V_{BE}+V_{CE,\min}$, where $V_{CE,\min}$ is the declared
forward-active/current-error margin rather than a universal saturation number.
It therefore needs about one additional base-emitter drop of output headroom
relative to a simple sink and retains mismatch, unequal-temperature,
finite-Early-voltage, capacitance, and bandwidth errors.

The shown $R_{\mathrm{REF}}$ also has finite incremental resistance. It must be
retained when evaluating the drawn circuit's output resistance; the estimate
in @eq-a03-wilson-output-resistance applies to the ideal current-driven
comparison. Feedback does not convert $R_{\mathrm{REF}}$ into a regulated
reference source. Under the same constant-junction-drop approximation, the
Wilson reference node is near $2V_{BE}$ and
$I_{\mathrm{REF}}\approx(V_{CC}-2V_{BE})/R_{\mathrm{REF}}$. The simple-mirror
expression in @eq-a03-bjt-mirror therefore cannot be reused, and supply or
junction-voltage changes still move the commanded current
[@analogdevices2021currentmirrors; @sedra2020microelectronic;
@wilson1968monolithic].

The comparison in @tbl-a03-mirror-selection places Wilson in context without
turning this chapter into an integrated-circuit design chapter.

| Mirror approach | Principal purpose | Cost or residual limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Simple two-device mirror | establish a shared-control current copy | first-order BJT base-current error; finite $r_o$; matching and compliance limits |
| Matched emitter/source degeneration | reduce sensitivity to device mismatch and raise output resistance | resistor area or parts; added voltage drop and noise |
| Widlar source | obtain a smaller output current without an impractically large reference resistor | nonlinear temperature-dependent ratio and extra compliance voltage |
| Wilson mirror | suppress first-order BJT base-current error and raise output resistance through feedback | extra device, internal node, headroom, and dynamic behavior |
| Cascode mirror | raise output resistance and reduce output-voltage modulation | additional biasing and substantial headroom |
| Wide-swing cascode variant | recover some compliance relative to an ordinary cascode | greater bias and topology complexity; matching remains |

: Selection map for improved mirrors. These are design directions, not
interchangeable precision sources. {#tbl-a03-mirror-selection}

Unmatched discrete transistors in either a simple or Wilson mirror should
therefore be treated as approximate bias elements, not precision sources. A07 owns full
small-signal derivations, MOS and bipolar variants, active loads, process
corners, matching, thermal gradients, and layout
[@analogdevices2021currentmirrors; @horowitz2015art;
@sedra2020microelectronic].

## Coupling, bypass, and device capacitances set frequency limits

### Coupling capacitors

An ideal coupling capacitor does not “pass AC” without qualification. Its
impedance is $1/(j\omega C)$, so it and the resistances seen from its terminals
form a high-pass response. For a capacitor between two Thévenin resistances
$R_A$ and $R_B$, with independent sources nulled and other capacitors treated
according to the stated approximation,

$$
f_p\approx\frac{1}{2\pi C(R_A+R_B)}.
$$ {#eq-a03-coupling-pole}

The sum appears because the capacitor sees a path through both sides. For the
input and output capacitors of @fig-a03-common-emitter,

$$
f_{\mathrm{IN}}\approx
\frac{1}{2\pi C_{\mathrm{IN}}
(R_{\mathrm{sig}}+R_{\mathrm{in}})},
$$ {#eq-a03-input-pole}

and

$$
f_{\mathrm{OUT}}\approx
\frac{1}{2\pi C_{\mathrm{OUT}}
(R_{\mathrm{out}}+R_L)}.
$$ {#eq-a03-output-pole}

Using $R_{\mathrm{out}}\parallel R_L$ in the second denominator would describe a
different topology and is a common error.

### Emitter bypass capacitor

$C_E$ in @fig-a03-common-emitter is across $R_{E2}$, not across the complete
emitter resistance. Its pole estimate uses the resistance seen between its own
terminals. With the source nulled and the transistor represented incrementally,
a useful first-order estimate is

$$
R_{C_E}\approx
R_{E2}\parallel
\left[
R_{E1}
+\frac{r_\pi+(R_{\mathrm{sig}}\parallel R_{\mathrm{TH}})}
{\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}+1}
\right],
$$ {#eq-a03-bypass-resistance}

so

$$
f_E\approx\frac{1}{2\pi R_{C_E}C_E}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bypass-pole}

At frequencies well below $f_E$, $C_E$ is ineffective and
$R_{E1}+R_{E2}$ degenerates the signal. Well above $f_E$, $R_{E2}$ is
approximately bypassed and gain magnitude rises. Unlike a coupling capacitor,
the bypass capacitor produces a gain shelf with a pole and a zero; it does not
force gain to zero at DC. If the input, output, and bypass transitions are
close, they cannot be treated as three independent pure high-pass factors.
Device interaction requires nodal analysis, simulation, or a sweep
[@sedra2020microelectronic].

### High-frequency capacitance and Miller multiplication

At higher frequency, BJT base-emitter and base-collector charge and MOSFET
gate-source and gate-drain charge add frequency-dependent terminal currents.
Let $C_\mu$ connect an inverting stage's input to output, and let
$A_v=\widetilde v_o/\widetilde v_i$. The input current through that capacitance
is

$$
\widetilde i_\mu
=j\omega C_\mu(\widetilde v_i-\widetilde v_o)
=j\omega C_\mu(1-A_v)\widetilde v_i.
$$

It therefore appears at the input as the approximate **Miller capacitance**

$$
C_{\mathrm{M,in}}\approx C_\mu(1-A_v).
$$ {#eq-a03-miller}

For negative gain, $1-A_v$ can be much larger than one. The input source
resistance and this enlarged effective capacitance can dominate the upper
corner. The approximation assumes one voltage gain over the frequency range in
question; as gain acquires frequency dependence, a complete nodal transfer
function is required.

The onsemi 2N3904 datasheet, for example, specifies maximum
$C_{obo}=4.0$ pF only at $V_{CB}=5.0$ V, $I_E=0$, and 100 kHz, and maximum
$C_{ibo}=8.0$ pF only at $V_{EB}=0.5$ V, $I_C=0$, and 100 kHz. Its minimum
$f_T=300$ MHz is specified at $I_C=10$ mA, $V_{CE}=20$ V, and 100 MHz
[@onsemi2n3904]. The electrical-characteristics table states
$T_A=25~^\circ$C unless otherwise noted. Those values prove neither the
hybrid-$\pi$ capacitances nor
the bandwidth of the 1 mA chapter stage. Bias, source resistance, load, Miller
multiplication, wiring, and probe capacitance still determine the assembled
response.

## Signal excursion and distortion bound usable gain

### AC load line and clipping

The DC load sees $R_C$, while an AC-coupled load makes the signal current see
$R_C\parallel R_L$. This distinction rotates the signal load line about the Q
point. Neglecting $r_o$, define

$$
R_{CL}=R_C\parallel R_L.
$$

The cutoff-side collector-voltage rise is approximately

$$
\widehat V_{o,\mathrm{cutoff}}
\approx I_{CQ}R_{CL},
$$ {#eq-a03-cutoff-headroom}

because collector current cannot decrease much below zero. The saturation-side
limit must include emitter movement and the minimum usable $V_{CE}$; with
$R_{E1}$ unbypassed,

$$
\widehat V_{o,\mathrm{sat}}
\approx
\frac{V_{CQ}-V_{EQ}-V_{CE,\min}}
{R_{CL}+R_{E1}}R_{CL}.
$$ {#eq-a03-saturation-headroom}

This is a piecewise large-signal screen, not a small-signal equation. The
nominal symmetrical peak cannot exceed the smaller of the two. A design equal
to that boundary has no margin, and the transistor becomes nonlinear before a
hard region boundary is reached.

For the illustrative stage,
$R_{CL}=3.197$ k$\Omega$ and
$I_{CQ}=1.064$ mA, so the cutoff-side estimate is 3.40 V peak. Taking
$V_{CE,\min}=0.20$ V only as an illustrative region screen gives a
saturation-side estimate near 5.3 V peak. Cutoff therefore limits nominal
symmetrical swing. This does not make 3.40 V peak a qualified distortion limit.

### Curvature and harmonics

For a BJT with $n=1$, substitute a sinusoidal
$\widetilde v_\pi=\widehat V_\pi\cos\omega t$ into the exponential and retain
terms through second order:

$$
\widetilde i_c
\approx
g_m\widehat V_\pi\cos\omega t
+\frac{I_{CQ}}{2V_T^2}
\widehat V_\pi^2\cos^2\omega t.
$$

Since $\cos^2\omega t=(1+\cos2\omega t)/2$, the approximate ratio of
second-harmonic peak current to fundamental peak current is

$$
\frac{\widehat I_2}{\widehat I_1}
\approx\frac{\widehat V_\pi}{4V_T}.
$$ {#eq-a03-bjt-second-harmonic}

The same quadratic term also shifts the mean current above the no-signal Q
value by

$$
\Delta I_{C,\mathrm{DC}}
\approx
\frac{I_{CQ}\widehat V_\pi^2}{4V_T^2}.
$$

This rectified DC term is why a nonlinear increment about the no-signal Q point
need not have zero mean. At 300 K, $\widehat V_\pi=5$ mV gives about 4.8%
second harmonic relative to the fundamental. This is a local
single-mechanism estimate, not the complete stage THD: emitter degeneration
changes the relationship between source voltage and $v_\pi$, the two signal
halves are not perfectly symmetric, higher orders remain, and clipping adds
strong harmonics. It does show why “only a few millivolts at the junction” can
matter.

## Noise and multistage loading bound usable performance

### Noise as a bandwidth-dependent floor

Noise is not one input offset. In the classical white-noise approximation, the
one-sided open-circuit, series-equivalent voltage amplitude spectral density of
a resistor at absolute temperature $T$ is

$$
e_{n,R}=\sqrt{4kTR}
\quad\left[\frac{\mathrm V}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}}\right].
$$ {#eq-a03-resistor-noise}

For a full Poisson stream of independent charge carriers, the one-sided
shot-noise current amplitude spectral density associated with average current
$I$ is

$$
i_{n,\mathrm{shot}}=\sqrt{2q_eI}
\quad\left[\frac{\mathrm A}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}}\right].
$$ {#eq-a03-shot-noise}

Correlations, partition, recombination, or circuit feedback can change the
terminal noise from this full-shot value
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

For $R=10.0$ k$\Omega$ at 300 K,
$e_{n,R}\approx12.9$ nV/$\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$. A flat density integrated over
noise-equivalent bandwidth $B$ contributes an RMS value proportional to
$\sqrt B$. Real transistor noise also includes frequency-dependent mechanisms,
and source resistance changes how current and voltage noise combine
[@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic].

The 2N3904's maximum 5.0 dB noise figure is specified only for
$I_C=100~\mu$A, $V_{CE}=5.0$ V, $R_S=1.0$ k$\Omega$, and 10 Hz to 15.7 kHz
at the table's stated $T_A=25~^\circ$C unless otherwise noted
[@onsemi2n3904]. It is not a universal input-noise density and does not qualify
the chapter's different bias point. A06 develops complete input-referred noise,
bandwidth, distortion, dynamic-range, and stability budgets.

### Multistage loading

Suppose stage 1 has open-circuit voltage gain $A_{v1,\mathrm{oc}}$ and output
resistance $R_{\mathrm{out1}}$, while stage 2 presents
$R_{\mathrm{in2}}$. The loaded first-stage gain is

$$
A_{v1,\mathrm{loaded}}
=A_{v1,\mathrm{oc}}
\frac{R_{\mathrm{in2}}}
{R_{\mathrm{out1}}+R_{\mathrm{in2}}}.
$$ {#eq-a03-interstage-loading}

Only after this loading is included may gains be cascaded:

$$
A_{v,\mathrm{total}}
=A_{v1,\mathrm{loaded}}A_{v2,\mathrm{loaded}}\cdots.
$$

If $R_{\mathrm{in2}}\rightarrow\infty$, the first stage approaches its
open-circuit gain. If $R_{\mathrm{in2}}=R_{\mathrm{out1}}$, the interstage
divider halves the first-stage output. A follower between stages can reduce
this loading at the cost of bias current, headroom, noise, and additional
frequency dependence.

## Evidence distinguishes bias, loading, and frequency faults

A wrong output waveform does not by itself localize a cause. The physical
circuit and its representations should be tested at discriminating points.
The hypotheses in @tbl-a03-diagnosis form a reasoning guide, not
measured evidence.

Use a disciplined localization sequence:

1. Verify supply, reference, current limit, and every stage's DC Q point before
   injecting an AC signal.
2. **Signal trace** from the input toward the output, comparing each named
   stage-boundary test point with a bounded prediction.
3. For a long chain, **split-half** by observing a safe midpoint. Continue in
   the faulty half rather than replacing parts throughout the system.
4. Change only one source, load, probe, amplitude, or frequency condition at a
   time; predict the direction of change before measuring.
5. Preserve the original failure state and raw observations until a
   discriminating test has actually rejected a hypothesis.

Signal tracing is intuitive when the input is known good; split-half can
localize a multistage fault with fewer observations. Neither method excuses DC
safety checks, probe-loading analysis, or stage-by-stage loading calculations
[@horowitz2015art; @malvino2021electronic].

| Observation | Competing hypotheses | Discriminating check |
|---|---|---|
| $V_C$ is near $V_{CC}$ with no signal | transistor cutoff; open emitter path; wrong pinout; base divider fault | measure TPB and emitter DC voltage relative to TP0, de-energize, then verify continuity and pinout |
| $V_C$ is near $V_E$ | excessive base bias; shorted collector path; wrong resistor; transistor saturation | compare TPB, emitter voltage, and current inferred independently from $R_C$ and $R_E$ |
| DC points agree but gain is low | load smaller than assumed; bypass capacitor ineffective; low $g_m$; probe loading | remove the load, compare gain above and below the predicted bypass corner, then change probe configuration |
| Low-frequency gain falls early | $C_{\mathrm{IN}}$, $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$, or $C_E$ too small; electrolytic degradation; resistance estimate wrong | vary one capacitor by a known factor and predict which corner should move |
| High-frequency gain falls early | Miller effect; source resistance; device capacitance; wiring or probe capacitance | reduce source resistance, use a lower-capacitance probe, and compare the direction and size of the change |
| One half-cycle clips first | asymmetric Q point; cutoff or saturation headroom; source offset | record TPB and TPC DC values, then compare positive and negative peak distances to the region boundaries |
| Gain drifts during warm-up | junction temperature; bias-network drift; supply drift; self-heating | record supply, TPB, emitter, collector, and case-temperature proxy versus time without attributing junction temperature directly |

: Hypothesis-driven amplifier diagnosis. Each row requires the same source,
load, amplitude, frequency, and instrument configuration for comparisons.
{#tbl-a03-diagnosis}

### Worked diagnosis: low gain with valid DC bias

Suppose an **illustrative synthetic record** for the canonical stage reports
$V_B=1.76$ V, $V_E=1.05$ V, $V_C=6.92$ V, and
$|A_v|=7.8$ at 1.00 kHz. These generated values teach reconciliation; they do
not qualify a physical amplifier. The terminal differences are

$$
V_{BE}=1.76-1.05=0.71~\mathrm V,
\qquad
V_{CE}=6.92-1.05=5.87~\mathrm V.
$$

Both are plausible for forward-active operation under the chapter
approximation. Independent resistor-path current estimates are

$$
I_C^{(R_C)}
=\frac{12.0-6.92}{4.70~\mathrm{k}\Omega}
=1.081~\mathrm{mA},
$$

and

$$
I_E^{(R_E)}
=\frac{1.05}{950~\Omega}
=1.105~\mathrm{mA}.
$$

Their 24 $\mu$A difference has the correct sign for positive base current,
although a quantitative closure test still needs resistor and voltage
uncertainty. The DC evidence therefore makes “wrong operating region” less
likely than an ineffective bypass or excess signal loading; it does not prove
either one.

The next discriminating observation is the 1.00 kHz signal at TPE relative to
TP0. A larger-than-predicted emitter signal supports an ineffective $C_E$
path. If TPE agrees, repeat the gain measurement first without $R_L$ and then
with a lower-capacitance probe. A gain recovery localizes loading more strongly
than replacing the transistor at random.

For current reconciliation, two independently obtained estimates can expose a
wiring or parameter error:

$$
I_C^{(R_C)}=\frac{V_{CC}-V_C}{R_C},
\qquad
I_E^{(R_E)}=\frac{V_E}{R_E}.
$$

Their difference is not expected to be zero because $I_E=I_C+I_B$, and each
estimate has measurement and resistor uncertainty. After base current and all
declared paths are assigned, a closure residual consistent with zero within
uncertainty supports the circuit description. Defining an unknown current as
the remainder would create an identity, not independent evidence.

## Worked design: a bounded 2N3904 voltage stage

### Requirements and assumptions

Design the circuit of @fig-a03-common-emitter as an extra-low-voltage teaching
stage. @tbl-a03-design-requirements states **illustrative requirements**; they
are not claims about an existing assembly.

| Quantity | Illustrative requirement and condition |
|---|---|
| Supply | $12.0$ V nominal, current limited to no more than 25 mA for the bench procedure |
| Source | $R_{\mathrm{sig}}=600~\Omega$; zero DC offset; sinusoidal amplitude stated as peak |
| Load | $R_L=10.0$ k$\Omega$ connected through $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$ |
| DC region | $V_{CEQ}>4.0$ V and $0.80~\mathrm V<V_{EQ}<1.25~\mathrm V$ |
| Midband gain | $9.0<|A_{v,\mathrm{sig}}|<11.5$ at 1.00 kHz and $\widehat V_{\mathrm{sig}}=50.0$ mV |
| Tested passband | at every point of a logarithmic 100 Hz–20.0 kHz sweep with spacing no coarser than 1/12 octave, gain remains within 1.0 dB of its 1.00 kHz value |
| Linearity | THD at TPO is less than 3.0% at 1.00 kHz and $\widehat V_o=0.50$ V |
| Large-signal margin | 1 dB gain-compression output amplitude exceeds 1.50 V peak at 1.00 kHz |

: Illustrative design requirements. Strict inequalities require guarded
measurement intervals to remain inside the stated boundaries.
{#tbl-a03-design-requirements}

Choose an onsemi 2N3904 and the nominal values in @tbl-a03-design-values.
Standard-value availability does not establish
tolerance, temperature coefficient, voltage coefficient, or capacitor
polarization; the actual parts must be specified before qualification.

| Element | Nominal choice | Function |
|---|---:|---|
| $R_1$ | 82.0 k$\Omega$ | upper bias-divider resistor |
| $R_2$ | 15.0 k$\Omega$ | lower bias-divider resistor |
| $R_C$ | 4.70 k$\Omega$ | collector load and voltage conversion |
| $R_{E1}$ | 270 $\Omega$ | unbypassed degeneration |
| $R_{E2}$ | 680 $\Omega$ | DC stabilization, bypassed at midband |
| $C_{\mathrm{IN}}$ | 1.00 $\mu$F | input DC isolation |
| $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$ | 1.00 $\mu$F | output DC isolation |
| $C_E$ | 47.0 $\mu$F | midband bypass of $R_{E2}$ |

: Nominal design choices. Values are calculation inputs, not measured
component values. {#tbl-a03-design-values}

The 2N3904 datasheet guarantees $h_{FE}\ge70$ at
$I_C=1.0$ mA and $V_{CE}=1.0$ V, while its 100–300 range applies at
$I_C=10$ mA and $V_{CE}=1.0$ V [@onsemi2n3904]. Both are pulse tests with
pulse width no greater than 300 $\mu$s and duty cycle no greater than 2%, in
the table stated at $T_A=25~^\circ$C unless otherwise noted. The 10 mA upper value must not
be presented as a guaranteed 1 mA range. The design therefore uses emitter
feedback and verifies the actual DC point rather than relying on one claimed
$\beta$.

### Executable nominal and sensitivity check

The following standard-library code reproduces the chapter arithmetic. It uses
the constant-$V_{BE}$ approximation, sweeps
$\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$, holds the distinct illustrative value
$\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}=100$ fixed, and neglects $r_o$. Its output is
**executable calculation evidence**, not simulation and not measurement.

```python
from math import log10, pi

vcc = 12.0
r1, r2 = 82e3, 15e3
rc, re1, re2 = 4.70e3, 270.0, 680.0
rl, r_sig = 10.0e3, 600.0
v_be, v_t = 0.700, 25.85e-3
c_in, c_out, c_e = 1.00e-6, 1.00e-6, 47.0e-6

v_th = vcc * r2 / (r1 + r2)
r_th = 1.0 / (1.0 / r1 + 1.0 / r2)
r_e_dc = re1 + re2
r_cl = 1.0 / (1.0 / rc + 1.0 / rl)

beta_ac = 100.0
print("beta_dc IC(mA)  VE(V)  VC(V)  VCE(V)  Av(sig)")
for beta_dc in (70.0, 100.0, 200.0):
    i_b = (v_th - v_be) / (r_th + (beta_dc + 1.0) * r_e_dc)
    i_c = beta_dc * i_b
    i_e = (beta_dc + 1.0) * i_b
    v_e = i_e * r_e_dc
    v_c = vcc - i_c * rc
    g_m = i_c / v_t
    r_pi = beta_ac / g_m
    a_vb = -g_m * r_cl / (1.0 + (g_m + 1.0 / r_pi) * re1)
    r_in_b = r_pi + (beta_ac + 1.0) * re1
    r_in = 1.0 / (1.0 / r_th + 1.0 / r_in_b)
    a_vsig = a_vb * r_in / (r_sig + r_in)
    print(
        f"{beta_dc:7.0f} {1e3*i_c:6.3f}  {v_e:5.3f}  "
        f"{v_c:5.3f}  {v_c-v_e:6.3f}  {a_vsig:7.3f}"
    )

beta_dc = 100.0
i_b = (v_th - v_be) / (r_th + (beta_dc + 1.0) * r_e_dc)
i_c = beta_dc * i_b
g_m = i_c / v_t
r_pi = beta_ac / g_m
r_in_b = r_pi + (beta_ac + 1.0) * re1
r_in = 1.0 / (1.0 / r_th + 1.0 / r_in_b)
r_base = 1.0 / (1.0 / r_sig + 1.0 / r_th)
r_ce = 1.0 / (
    1.0 / re2
    + 1.0 / (re1 + (r_pi + r_base) / (beta_ac + 1.0))
)

for name, resistance, capacitance in (
    ("input", r_sig + r_in, c_in),
    ("output", rc + rl, c_out),
    ("bypass", r_ce, c_e),
):
    corner = 1.0 / (2.0 * pi * resistance * capacitance)
    print(f"{name:7s} pole estimate = {corner:5.2f} Hz")


def solve_3x3(matrix, vector):
    augmented = [
        row[:] + [value]
        for row, value in zip(matrix, vector)
    ]
    for column in range(3):
        pivot = max(
            range(column, 3),
            key=lambda row: abs(augmented[row][column]),
        )
        augmented[column], augmented[pivot] = (
            augmented[pivot],
            augmented[column],
        )
        divisor = augmented[column][column]
        for index in range(column, 4):
            augmented[column][index] /= divisor
        for row in range(3):
            if row == column:
                continue
            factor = augmented[row][column]
            for index in range(column, 4):
                augmented[row][index] -= (
                    factor * augmented[column][index]
                )
    return [augmented[row][3] for row in range(3)]


def gain_at(frequency):
    omega = 2.0 * pi * frequency
    z_in = r_sig + 1.0 / (1j * omega * c_in)
    y_in = 1.0 / z_in
    z_emitter = re1 + 1.0 / (
        1.0 / re2 + 1j * omega * c_e
    )
    z_output = rl + 1.0 / (1j * omega * c_out)
    y_bias = 1.0 / r1 + 1.0 / r2
    matrix = [
        [y_in + y_bias + 1.0 / r_pi, -1.0 / r_pi, 0.0],
        [
            -1.0 / r_pi - g_m,
            1.0 / z_emitter + 1.0 / r_pi + g_m,
            0.0,
        ],
        [g_m, -g_m, 1.0 / rc + 1.0 / z_output],
    ]
    _, _, v_collector = solve_3x3(matrix, [y_in, 0.0, 0.0])
    return v_collector * rl / z_output


ratio = abs(gain_at(100.0) / gain_at(1000.0))
ratio_db = 20.0 * log10(ratio)
print(f"100 Hz / 1 kHz = {ratio:.4f} ({ratio_db:.3f} dB)")
```

Expected output:

```text
beta_dc IC(mA)  VE(V)  VC(V)  VCE(V)  Av(sig)
     70  1.010  0.973  7.255   6.282  -10.041
    100  1.064  1.021  7.000   5.979  -10.084
    200  1.135  1.084  6.665   5.581  -10.135
input   pole estimate = 16.78 Hz
output  pole estimate = 10.83 Hz
bypass  pole estimate = 16.28 Hz
100 Hz / 1 kHz = 0.9645 (-0.314 dB)
```

The table teaches sensitivity within the declared approximation: a nearly
three-to-one illustrative $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ change moves calculated current
by about 12% and source gain by about 0.94% while
$\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$ is held fixed. It does not predict a production
distribution, because $V_{BE}$, $\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$, $r_o$, temperature, and
component values were not jointly varied.

### Frequency and headroom screens

The three estimated low-frequency poles lie near one another. If they were
independent pure high-pass factors, multiplication would misrepresent the
bypass shelf. Evaluating the complete nominal hybrid-$\pi$ network gives a
100 Hz-to-1 kHz gain ratio of about 0.965, or $-0.31$ dB. This remains
calculation evidence under assumed $g_m$, $r_\pi$, ideal capacitors, and
$r_o\rightarrow\infty$; SPICE analysis with an identified device description or
a sweep is needed before asserting the physical lower cutoff.

The datasheet's conditional $f_T$ and capacitance limits make 20 kHz plausible
but do not prove the upper-band requirement at this different bias. The
high-frequency acceptance sweep must include the actual source, 10 k$\Omega$
load, wiring, and probes. A simulation is useful calculated evidence only if its
device description, temperature, parasitics, source, load, and probe
capacitance are recorded.

The nominal cutoff-side headroom is 3.40 V peak, above the 1.50 V
gain-compression requirement. That comparison is only a feasibility screen:
gain compression can occur before cutoff, and the result has not carried
tolerances or temperature. The 0.50 V peak linearity test asks for only about
50 mV peak at the source under the nominal gain, but the THD requirement still
requires simulation or measurement.

### DC power screen

At the nominal Q point, collector-port transistor dissipation is approximately

$$
P_{CEQ}=V_{CEQ}I_{CQ}
\approx(5.979~\mathrm V)(1.064~\mathrm{mA})
=6.36~\mathrm{mW}.
$$

Base-port power is much smaller in the nominal approximation but is not
identically zero. The collector-resistor dissipation is

$$
P_{R_C}=I_{CQ}^2R_C\approx5.32~\mathrm{mW},
$$

and the combined emitter-resistor dissipation is

$$
P_{R_E}=I_{EQ}^2(R_{E1}+R_{E2})\approx1.10~\mathrm{mW}.
$$

The loaded base voltage is $V_{BQ}\approx1.721$ V, so the mandatory divider
resistors dissipate

$$
P_{R_1}=\frac{(V_{CC}-V_{BQ})^2}{R_1}\approx1.29~\mathrm{mW},
\qquad
P_{R_2}=\frac{V_{BQ}^2}{R_2}\approx0.197~\mathrm{mW}.
$$

The nominal base-port transistor power is
$V_{BEQ}I_{BQ}\approx7.45~\mu$W. The total supply current is approximately
$I_{CQ}+(V_{CC}-V_{BQ})/R_1=1.19$ mA.

These nominal values are far below a 0.125 W resistor rating, but a final parts
decision still needs actual resistor ratings, tolerance, ambient condition,
spacing, and fault behavior. The transistor's 625 mW and
$R_{\theta JA}=200~^\circ$C/W figures are listed for the TO-92 device at
$T_A=25~^\circ$C; dissipation derates above 25 °C at 5.0 mW/°C, maximum
ratings are based on $T_J=150~^\circ$C, and the datasheet identifies them as
steady-state limits [@onsemi2n3904]. They cannot be multiplied into a universal
junction-temperature guarantee. The 25 mA source limit also
bounds many wiring faults to a much lower energy than an unrestricted supply.

If polarized capacitors are used, $C_{\mathrm{IN}}$ normally has its positive
terminal toward TPB, $C_{\mathrm{OUT}}$ toward TPC, and $C_E$ toward the top of
$R_{E2}$ under the stated DC conditions. Reverse or bipolar signal conditions
can invalidate that choice; use a suitable non-polar or bipolar part when
polarity cannot be maintained.

### Requirement disposition before measurement

The calculation does not justify an overall pass. @tbl-a03-disposition makes
the evidence gap explicit.

| Requirement | Evidence reached | Disposition before build |
|---|---|---|
| DC region | nominal calculation and illustrative $\beta_{\mathrm{DC}}$ sweep; $V_{BE}$, tolerance, and temperature corners incomplete | feasible, not qualified |
| 1 kHz gain | nominal incremental calculation with fixed $\beta_{\mathrm{ac}}$ and $r_o\rightarrow\infty$ | provisional calculation pass |
